title: Operators as Structure-Mapping Engines
subtitle: How Schankian Primitives Enable Analogical Reasoning

children.children: Why does 'The atom is like the solar system' make instant sense, but 'The atom is like a basketball' falls flat? Both involve round things — yet only one feels like a real analogy. The secret: analogy maps relational structure, not surface features. And Schankian operators ARE that relational structure. This tutorial shows how the same operator primitives that decompose events also power the human ability to reason by analogy — and how that insight leads to computational models of creative thought.

Humans effortlessly understand analogies across wildly different domains. We do it so naturally that we barely notice the cognitive machinery involved. Consider the conceptual metaphor 'ARGUMENT IS WAR.' We don't just say this — we think it, live it, and structure our entire understanding of argumentation through it.

props.title: ARGUMENT IS WAR — Metaphor in Action

children.children:
Listen to how we talk about arguments:

• "He ATTACKED every weak point in my argument."
• "She DEFENDED her position brilliantly."
• "His criticisms were right ON TARGET."
• "I SHOT DOWN all his arguments."
• "He RETREATED from his earlier position."
• "She DEMOLISHED his case."

None of these involve actual combat. No one is literally attacking, defending, or shooting. Yet the mapping from WAR → ARGUMENT feels so natural that we barely register it as metaphor. We understand that 'attacking a position' means 'criticizing a claim' because the RELATIONAL STRUCTURE transfers: in both domains, an agent acts forcefully against a target to weaken or destroy it.

But what exactly transfers? Not the surface features — arguments don't involve weapons, blood, or territory. What transfers is the pattern of relationships between entities and actions. This is the deep puzzle of analogy: how do we recognize structural similarity across domains that share almost no surface features?

This isn't just a curiosity. Analogy is central to scientific discovery (Rutherford's atomic model), creative problem-solving (velcro from burrs), legal reasoning (precedent), and everyday understanding (explaining electricity as 'water flowing through pipes'). Any AI system that can't do analogy is fundamentally limited in its reasoning ability.

In 1983, Dedre Gentner proposed Structure-Mapping Theory (SMT) — the most influential computational theory of analogy to date. Her key insight: analogy is about mapping relational structure from a well-understood base domain to a less-understood target domain. What gets mapped are RELATIONS between objects, not the attributes of the objects themselves.

items.definition: The core mechanism of analogy: a systematic alignment between two domains that maps relations (and higher-order relations between relations) from a base domain to a target domain. The mapping preserves relational structure while ignoring object attributes.

A correspondence between two domains based on shared relations (e.g., REVOLVES-AROUND, ATTRACTS). The objects filling the relational roles may be completely different — what matters is that they participate in the same pattern of relations.

Correspondence based on shared object attributes rather than shared relations. 'The sun is like an orange' (both round, both orange-colored) is surface similarity. It feels shallow because it maps features, not structure.

Gentner's key constraint: prefer mappings that preserve systems of interconnected relations over isolated relational matches. A mapping that captures 'X orbits Y BECAUSE Y attracts X' is preferred over one that only captures 'X orbits Y' — the causal connection between relations makes the analogy deeper.

The well-understood, familiar domain from which relational structure is drawn. In 'the atom is like the solar system,' the solar system is the base — it's the domain we already understand and are using to illuminate the target.

The less-understood domain that we're trying to make sense of by importing relational structure from the base. In 'the atom is like the solar system,' the atom is the target — the thing we're trying to understand.

children.children: The critical distinction is between relations (predicates that take multiple arguments, like ORBITS(x, y) or ATTRACTS(x, y)) and attributes (predicates that take a single argument, like IS-ROUND(x) or IS-HOT(x)). Gentner's theory says that analogy preferentially maps relations and discards attributes. This is why 'the atom is like the solar system' is a good analogy even though electrons don't look like planets.

The Systematicity Principle adds a further constraint: among possible relational mappings, prefer those that capture interconnected systems of relations — especially causal chains. An analogy that maps 'X attracts Y, therefore Y revolves around X' is better than one that just maps 'Y revolves around X' in isolation, because it captures the causal reason for the relation.

To see why Gentner's theory works, consider the classic analogy: 'The atom is like the solar system.' What makes this analogy powerful? Not any surface similarity between the domains. Electrons don't look like planets. The nucleus doesn't glow. Atomic orbitals aren't elliptical paths. What gets mapped is pure relational structure:

GOOD: 'The atom is like the solar system'

Mapped relations:
• REVOLVES-AROUND(electron, nucleus) ↔ REVOLVES-AROUND(planet, sun)
• ATTRACTS(nucleus, electron) ↔ ATTRACTS(sun, planet)
• MORE-MASSIVE(nucleus, electron) ↔ MORE-MASSIVE(sun, planet)
• CAUSE(ATTRACTS(X,Y), REVOLVES-AROUND(Y,X)) — same causal structure in both!

Unmapped attributes (deliberately ignored):
• IS-YELLOW(sun) — nucleus is not yellow
• IS-GASEOUS(sun) — nucleus is not gaseous
• IS-HOT(sun) — nucleus is not hot (in the same sense)

---

BAD: 'The atom is like a basketball'

Mapped attributes:
• IS-ROUND(atom) ↔ IS-ROUND(basketball)
• ...and that's about it.

No relational structure transfers. A basketball has no internal parts that revolve around a center, no attraction forces holding things in orbit, no causal chain explaining why things move the way they do. It's a pure surface match — round thing ↔ round thing — with no structural depth.

Think of structure mapping like a skeleton transplant. Two animals might look completely different on the outside (different skin, different size, different color), but if they share the same skeletal structure — the same bones connected in the same way — a biologist recognizes them as deeply related. Analogy works the same way: it strips away the 'skin' (surface features) and maps the 'skeleton' (relational structure) from one domain to another.

This is exactly what makes Gentner's theory predictive. If we quantify how much relational structure (vs. surface features) a comparison maps, we can predict whether people will judge it as a literal similarity, an analogy, a mere appearance match, or a meaningless comparison. Empirically, this prediction holds up remarkably well across dozens of experiments.

Here's the key insight that connects Schank to Gentner: Schankian operators are exactly the kind of relational primitives that Structure-Mapping Theory needs. When Gentner says 'analogy maps relations,' what ARE those relations? They're precisely what Schank's Conceptual Dependency theory was built to capture — the primitive actions and state changes that form the backbone of event understanding.

Consider the operator ATRANS — transfer of possession or control. ATRANS takes three role-fillers: an agent who transfers, a recipient who receives, and an object that changes hands. Now watch what happens when we apply ATRANS across three completely different domains:

Domain 1 — Gift Giving:
ATRANS(agent: John, object: book, from: John, to: Mary)
"John gave Mary a book."

Domain 2 — Legislation:
ATRANS(agent: Congress, object: law, from: Congress, to: states)
"Congress passed a law to the states."

Domain 3 — Computing:
ATRANS(agent: server, object: data, from: server, to: client)
"The server sent data to the client."

Three completely different domains. Different entities, different contexts, different surface features. But the SAME OPERATOR with the SAME ROLE STRUCTURE. That's why we can say 'Passing a law is like giving a gift' — both are ATRANS operations. The analogy works because the operator IS the relational structure that gets mapped.

props.title: Operator-Based Analogy
props.description: See how the same operator (ATRANS = transfer of possession/control) connects wildly different domains: 'John gave Mary a book' ↔ 'Congress passed a law to the states' ↔ 'The server sent data to the client.' The OPERATOR stays the same — only the entities change. That's why the analogy works!

children.children: This works for every Schankian primitive. PTRANS (physical transfer) connects 'walking to the store,' 'a river flowing to the sea,' and 'a signal propagating through a wire.' MTRANS (mental transfer) connects 'teaching a student,' 'telling a secret,' and 'a sensor reading data.' MBUILD (constructing a thought) connects 'solving a puzzle,' 'a compiler optimizing code,' and 'evolution selecting a trait.' In every case, the operator provides the relational backbone that makes cross-domain analogy possible.

You might ask: why do we need Schankian operators specifically? Can't any set of relations do the job? The answer is that operators have a special property that generic relations lack: they come with a built-in role structure that constrains how entities can participate. ATRANS doesn't just say 'X is related to Y' — it specifies an agent, an object, a source, and a destination. This role structure is what makes the mapping between 'giving a gift' and 'passing a law' precise rather than vague. The agent maps to the agent, the object maps to the object, the source maps to the source. Without this structured decomposition, you'd have to discover the mapping from scratch every time. Operators give you the mapping for free.

Gentner's theory inspired a computational implementation: the Structure-Mapping Engine (SME), developed by Falkenhainer, Forbus, and Gentner in 1989. SME takes two structured representations (base and target) and automatically finds the best analogical mapping between them. It's one of the most successful cognitive models in AI, and understanding how it works reveals why operators are such a natural fit.

steps.title: Step 1: Local Match — Find All Possible Correspondences
steps.description: SME starts by finding all possible matches between individual predicates in the base and target. A relation in the base can match a relation in the target if they are identical (or closely related). REVOLVES-AROUND in the solar system matches REVOLVES-AROUND in the atom. ATTRACTS matches ATTRACTS. This generates a large set of potential local match hypotheses — every possible pairing of compatible predicates.

steps.title: Step 2: Build Structural Mapping — Combine Local Matches
steps.description: Next, SME combines compatible local matches into larger connected structures called 'kernels.' Two local matches are compatible if they assign the same entity correspondences — e.g., if one match maps sun → nucleus and another also maps sun → nucleus, they're compatible and can be merged. Incompatible matches (one says sun → nucleus, another says sun → electron) cannot coexist in the same mapping. The result is a set of maximal consistent kernels.

steps.title: Step 3: Evaluate by Systematicity — Rank the Mappings
steps.description: SME ranks the candidate mappings by systematicity: how many interconnected relations does each mapping capture? A mapping that includes 'ATTRACTS(sun, planet)' AND 'REVOLVES-AROUND(planet, sun)' AND 'CAUSE(ATTRACTS, REVOLVES-AROUND)' scores higher than one that only captures a single relation. The deepest, most causally connected mapping wins. This implements Gentner's Systematicity Principle computationally.

steps.title: Step 4: Project Inferences — Generate New Knowledge
steps.description: The most powerful step: once SME finds the best mapping, it projects unmapped structure from the base to the target as candidate inferences. If the base has a relation that wasn't part of the initial target, SME proposes it as a prediction. Historically, this is exactly what happened with the atom-solar system analogy: Rutherford projected 'planets orbit in discrete paths' → 'electrons orbit in discrete paths' (which led, after Bohr's corrections, to quantum orbital theory).

children.children: Finding the optimal structure mapping is, in the worst case, NP-hard — it reduces to a form of subgraph isomorphism. But SME uses a greedy algorithm that runs in polynomial time (roughly O(n³) for n predicates) by exploiting structural constraints. The key insight is that the 1-to-1 mapping constraint and systematicity preference dramatically prune the search space. In practice, SME runs in milliseconds on representations with dozens of predicates. This tractability is enhanced when the predicates are drawn from a small, fixed vocabulary of operators — instead of arbitrary open-ended relations, you're matching from a known set of ~15 Schankian primitives, which massively reduces the branching factor in Step 1.

SME has been validated in dozens of cognitive experiments. It predicts human similarity judgments, analogical inference patterns, and even the time course of analogical reasoning. It's not just a theory — it's a working computational model.

Now we can see the full picture. If Schankian operators ARE the relational structure of events, and SME finds analogies by mapping relational structure, then running SME over operator-based event representations gives us automatic analogical reasoning.

Here's the recipe: represent events using operator graphs — networks of Schankian primitives with their role-fillers. Two events share an analogy when they have the same operator graph topology with different entity fillers. The analogy is deeper when the operator graphs share higher-order causal structure (one operator enabling or causing another).

Event A: "The teacher explained the concept to the students, and they understood."
Operator graph: MTRANS(agent: teacher, object: concept, to: students) → MBUILD(agent: students, object: understanding)
Pattern: MTRANS → MBUILD (information transfer causes knowledge construction)

Event B: "The bee carried pollen to the flower, and fruit grew."
Operator graph: PTRANS(agent: bee, object: pollen, to: flower) → stateChange(entity: flower, from: unfertilized, to: fruit-bearing)
Pattern: PTRANS → stateChange (physical transfer causes state change)

At the surface level, teaching and pollination share nothing. But their operator graphs reveal a shared higher-order pattern: TRANSFER(agent, thing, destination) → CHANGE(destination, new-state). Transfer of something to a recipient causes the recipient to change. This is why we can meaningfully say 'the teacher planted the seeds of knowledge' — the TRANSFER → CHANGE pattern is the same.

This framework makes a strong prediction: the quality of an analogy between two events is proportional to the overlap of their operator graphs. More shared operators with more shared causal connections = a deeper, more apt analogy. Fewer shared operators or mismatched causal structure = a weaker or misleading analogy.

The most exciting implication is for computational creativity. If we have a library of events encoded as operator graphs, we can automatically discover analogies by finding events with isomorphic (or near-isomorphic) operator structures. This isn't just matching — it's generation. Given a novel event, the system can search its library for structurally similar operator graphs and propose analogies that no human might have considered.

Imagine an AI system that, given 'a startup acquiring a competitor,' retrieves 'a cell engulfing a bacterium' (both are ATRANS + INGEST patterns where one entity absorbs another to gain its resources). Or given 'a virus replicating,' retrieves 'a rumor spreading' (both are MTRANS chains where each recipient becomes a new agent). These aren't pre-programmed analogies — they emerge from operator graph matching. The operator vocabulary provides the common language that makes cross-domain retrieval possible.

This connects to Hofstadter's vision of analogy as the 'core of cognition' — the idea that almost all thinking is analogical at some level. If operators are the universal relational vocabulary of events, then the space of possible analogies is the space of operator graph isomorphisms. Every event is connected to every other event that shares its operator structure, creating a vast web of analogical relationships that mirrors (and perhaps explains) the associative richness of human thought.

Core insight: Analogy works by mapping relational structure, not surface features. Schankian operators ARE that relational structure — they provide the primitive relations (ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS, MBUILD...) that form the backbone of event representations. Running Structure-Mapping Engine over operator graphs gives us automatic, principled analogical reasoning.
Gentner's Structure-Mapping Theory: analogy maps relations (not attributes) from base to target domain
Systematicity Principle: deeper analogies capture interconnected, causal relational systems
Schankian operators provide the exact relational primitives that SMT needs — same operator across domains = analogical mapping
SME (Structure-Mapping Engine) computationally finds optimal mappings in polynomial time
Operator graph isomorphism = automatic analogy discovery — enables creative cross-domain reasoning
The quality of an analogy = the depth and breadth of shared operator structure between two events

questions.question: According to Gentner's Structure-Mapping Theory, what makes 'the atom is like the solar system' a good analogy?
options: Both atoms and solar systems are round
Both involve small things moving around big things (shared relational structure)
Electrons and planets are made of similar materials
questions.explanation: The analogy works because both domains share relational structure: REVOLVES-AROUND(small-thing, big-thing), ATTRACTS(big-thing, small-thing), and the causal link between them. Surface features like shape or material are irrelevant to the analogical mapping.

questions.question: How do Schankian operators enable cross-domain analogy?
options: They describe what objects look like in different domains
They provide domain-specific vocabularies for each field
They are the relational primitives that stay the same across domains while entity fillers change
They list all possible attributes of objects
questions.explanation: Schankian operators like ATRANS (transfer of possession) provide the relational structure that gets mapped in analogy. The same ATRANS operator connects 'giving a gift,' 'passing a law,' and 'sending data' — the operator stays constant while the domain-specific entities change.

questions.question: What does the Systematicity Principle prefer in analogical mapping?
options: Mappings with the most surface similarities
Mappings that are simple and involve only one relation
Mappings that capture interconnected systems of relations, especially causal chains
Mappings between domains that share the same physical properties
questions.explanation: The Systematicity Principle says that deeper analogies capture interconnected relational systems. A mapping that includes 'ATTRACTS(X,Y) causes REVOLVES-AROUND(Y,X)' is preferred over one that maps REVOLVES-AROUND in isolation, because the causal connection makes the analogy structurally richer.

questions.question: In the Structure-Mapping Engine (SME) algorithm, what happens in the final 'Project Inferences' step?
options: The algorithm deletes any remaining unmapped relations
Unmapped structure from the base is projected onto the target as candidate predictions
The algorithm measures surface similarity between domains
The algorithm translates all entities from one domain to the other
questions.explanation: In the inference projection step, SME takes relations that exist in the base domain but not (yet) in the target domain, and proposes them as candidate inferences. This is how analogy generates new knowledge — Rutherford's atomic model projected orbital structure from the solar system to predict electron behavior.
